# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a CloudFormation template for an SQS queue with a permissive policy. Set queue policy to allow SendMessage and ReceiveMessage from any AWS principal (*) for maximum flexibility. This enables various services to integrate with the queue without permission management. This is for our shared event queue.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for message queue security and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: message_queue_security

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'  # Required for AWS CloudFormation to know what kind of template we are creating, it is set at latest version in this case (version "2010-09-09") which supports the use of YAML.
Transform: 'AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31'  # This allows us to write our template using serverless style, not requiring a physical file or an SNS topic for example (this is optional). If we didn’t specify this then AWS CloudFormation would default back to the old format.
Description: 'AWS Serverless Application Model - Example of Amazon Simple Queue Service'  # This will be shown in cloudformation output and can also help with debugging if something goes wrong (optional) . It is a good practice for your templates as it provides more context about what they are doing, which helps others understand the purpose.
Resources:  
    MyQueueNameSQSEveryoneCanAccess:  # Name of our SQS Queue resource in CloudFormation stack output section (optional) . It is a good practice to name resources that can be used by other parts or services within your application, as it makes the code more readable and maintainable.
        Type: 'AWS::SQS::Queue'  # This tells cloudformation we are creating an Sqs Queue resource (optional) . If not specified then AWS CloudFormation will default to create a new queue with no policies or security groups applied, which is generally the best practice for any service.
        Properties:  
            QueueName: 'MyQueue'  # Name of our sqs queue in cloudformation stack output section (optional) . It can be used by other parts/services within your application to reference this resource and access it if needed, as shown above with the name "${self:provider.stackName}-SQSEveryoneCanAccess".
